{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}

No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin University COE Program: Shinto and Shrine Historical Archives. {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiyanome Shrine}}[[Category:Ōmiya Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmiya No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin University COE Program: Shinto and Shrine Historical Archives. {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiyanome Shrine}}[[Category:Ōmiya Shrine]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmiya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin University COE Program: Shinto and Shrine Historical Archives. {{DEFAULTSORT:Omiyanome Shrine}}[[Category:Ōmiya Shrine]][[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmiya Shrine]][[Category:qq]] The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==

{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto Prefectural Designations === * Historic Site: Shrine precinct (designated 1986) === Kyōtango City Designations === * Tangible Cultural Property: Former Main Hall (Edo period, 1695) == Related Articles == * {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}} * {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} == External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/370401.html Ōmiyame Shrine] - Kokugakuin revision.comment: Text replacement - "名神大社}}" to "名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}}"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ōmiyame Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|280px]]
Main hall
(With stone lanterns, Important Cultural Properties, in front) |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳|大宮町周枳}} |Latitude = 35.35.28.37 |Longitude = 135.6.6.73 |ISO = JP-26 |deity = {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}
Wakamiya-no-Kami |ShrineRank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}} 2 seats)
(Traditionally) Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}
Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}} |Establishment = Unknown |AnnualFestival = October 10 |MainHall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |Othernames = Ōmiyame Shrine・Suki Shrine・Suki Palace |LabelPosition = top |Map = Japan Kyoto }} [[File:Omiyame-jinja, torii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Torii Gate}}]] '''Ōmiyame Shrine''' (おおみやめじんじゃ/おおみやのめじんじゃ, '''Ōmiyame Shrine''') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}}, {{ill|Kyōtango|ja|京丹後市}}, {{ill|Suki|ja|周枳}}. It is a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社}}) and is traditionally known as the Second Shrine of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国}}. Under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was classified as a {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|府社}}. Historically, it was also referred to as "Suki Shrine" or "Suki Palace." == Overview == Ōmiyame Shrine is located on a terrace on the west slope of Mount Kizumi, upstream of the Takenogawa River in northern Kyoto Prefecture. Its deities are {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} and Wakamiya-no-Kami. It is the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} to enshrine Ōmiyame-no-Kami outside the Imperial Court, and it is sometimes considered the original shrine of Ōmiyame-no-Kami worshipped at the court. The shrine precinct is also an archaeological site, yielding numerous artifacts from the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} onward. Notably, mid-Kofun period ritual artifacts have been discovered, confirming the site's development from an ancient ritual ground into a Shinto shrine. In addition to the shrine's unique deity and its transition from primitive to Ritsuryō-period worship, the site is of great historical and archaeological significance. The precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site in 1981. Two stone lanterns are designated as National Important Cultural Properties, and the shrine's old main hall is a Tangō City-designated Tangible Cultural Property. Local performing arts, such as the Three Role Dance, Sasa-bayashi, and Kagura, were also designated as Intangible Cultural Properties of Kyoto Prefecture in 1981 and of Kyōtango City the same year. == Deities ==
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;text-align:center;font-size:85%;" |+{{ill|Jingikan|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=}} Western Court
{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden}} Deities !Hall!!deity |- |First Hall||{{ill|Kamimusuhi|ja|カミムスビ}} |- |Second Hall||{{ill|Takamimusuhi|ja|タカミムスビ}} |- |Third Hall||Tama-tsumu-musuhi |- |Fourth Hall||Ikumusubi |- |Fifth Hall||Ashimusubi |- |Sixth Hall||{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}} |- |Seventh Hall||Miketsu |- |Eighth Hall||{{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主}} |}
The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine pamphlet.: * '''{{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|オオミヤノメ|Ōmiyame-no-Kami}}''' * '''Wakamiya-no-Kami''' The {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} lists two seats for Ōmiyame Shrine, and one of the deities, Ōmiyame-no-Kami, is well-known for being enshrined at the Imperial Court as one of the Eight Hasshinden Deities. No other shrine bearing the name Ōmiyame exists, making this the only {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} enshrining the deity outside the court. == History == === Establishment === The exact establishment of the shrine is unknownŌmiyame Shrine, Heibonsha, 1981.. Archaeological evidence suggests the area was a settlement from the Yayoi period, later becoming a ritual site during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} and eventually a shrine during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. === Modern Period === After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was classified as a Village Shrine in 1873 and later promoted to Prefectural Shrine in 1924. In 1986, the precinct was designated a Kyoto Prefectural Historic Site. == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties (National) === * Two stone lanterns (Kamakura period) === Kyoto
